1: hôy ʾAriʾēl ʾAriʾēl qiryat ḥānâ Dāwid = "Woe, Ariel, Ariel, the city where David camped." ʾAriʾēl (H740) carries a double meaning: (1) "lion of God" (ʾărî + ʾēl) — the city as a powerful beast; (2) "hearth of God" or "altar-hearth" (ʾar-ʾēl, cf. Ezekiel 43:15-16 where the altar hearth is called haʾăriʾēl) — Jerusalem as the city of the divine altar. The irony: the city that performs sacrifices will itself become a sacrificial hearth surrounded by siege works (v3). The double meaning is sustained throughout the oracle.
2: waăṣartî ʿal-ʾAriʾēl wĕhāyĕtâ tĕʾăniyyâ waʾĕniyyâ = "I will press hard upon Ariel (ṣûr, to besiege/distress), and she will be full of lamentation (tĕʾăniyyâ) and moaning (ʾăniyyâ)." The wordplay on ʾAriʾēl continues: wĕhāyĕtâ lî kĕʾAriʾēl = "and she shall be to me like an Ariel" — the city becomes the thing its name signifies. The siege transforms Jerusalem into the hearth/altar of divine judgment, just as its altar was the hearth of sacrifice.
3: wĕḥānîtî ʿālayik dûr = "I will encamp against you as a circle (dûr)" — the siege works surrounding Jerusalem. wĕṣartî ʿālayik māṣâb = "and I will besiege you with a siege mound (māṣâb)." wăhăqîmōtî ʿālayik mĕṣurōt = "and I will raise up siege towers (mĕṣurōt) against you." Three different siege-work terms — the vocabulary of ancient military engineering — applied to YHWH attacking his own city. The enemy, whoever it is militarily, is YHWH's instrument.
4: wĕšāpalt mittĕreṣ tĕdabbērî = "you will be brought low, you will speak from the ground (ʾereṣ)" — humiliation described as speaking from the earth rather than upright. ûmēʿāpār tiŝpeh ʾimrātek = "and your speech (ʾimrâ) will come muffled from the dust." kĕʾôḇ mēʾereṣ qôlēk = "like a ghost/spirit-medium (ʾôḇ) from the ground, your voice" — the defeated city speaks like a necromantic spirit, a murmur from below ground. The city that pursued the dead (cf. Isaiah 8:19) will speak like the dead.
5: wĕhāyâ kĕʾāḇāq dak hămôn zārāyik = "and the multitude (hāmôn) of your foes will become like fine dust (ʾāḇāq dak)" — the sudden reversal: the siege armies that reduced Jerusalem to a whisper will themselves become dust. ûkĕmōṣ ʿōḇēr hămôn ʿārîṣîm = "and the multitude of the ruthless like blowing chaff (môṣ ʿōḇēr)" — the judgment works both ways: Jerusalem humbled, then enemies scattered. The eschatological double reversal.
6: mēʿim YHWH ṣĕḇāʾôt tiPPāqēd = "from YHWH of armies she will be visited (pāqad)" — pāqad (H6485) = to visit in attention, either for blessing or judgment; here YHWH's visitation brings sudden reversal. bĕraʿam ûbĕraʿaš wĕqôl gādôl sûpâ ûsĕʿārâ wĕlahab ʾēš ʾōkēlâ = "with thunder, earthquake, great noise, storm, tempest, and devouring flame" — the full theophanic toolkit (cf. Exodus 19:16-18; 1 Kings 19:11-12). The reversal from siege to salvation comes through direct divine intervention in creation.
7: wĕhāyâ kĕḥălôm ḥăzôn laylâ hămôn kol-haggôyim = "and the multitude of all the nations that fight against Ariel will be like a dream, a night vision." The dreamer wakes and the threat is gone. wĕkol-ṣōbĕhêhā ûmĕṣādôtêhā wĕhammaṣîq lāh = "all who fight against her and her fortification and those who press her hard." Three classes of attacker — all become a vanished dream at YHWH's intervention.
8: wĕhāyâ kaʾăšer yaḥălōm hārāʿēḇ wĕhinnēh ʾōkēl = "as when a hungry man dreams he is eating, and awakens with his hunger unsatisfied (rêqâ napšô, lit. his soul is empty)." The enemy armies that dreamed of conquering Jerusalem will wake to find their campaign was illusory. The simile of the thirsty man who dreams of drinking (second image) reinforces: the apparent victory over Jerusalem will evaporate like a dream at sunrise.
9: hitmahmĕhû wĕtĕmāhû hiššāʿăšĕʿû wĕšōʿû = "Be dull and be amazed, blind yourselves and be blind" — four imperatives of stupor, possibly ironic: Isaiah commands the people to do what they are already doing. šāḵrû wĕlōʾ yāyin nāʿû wĕlōʾ šēkār = "they are drunk but not with wine, they stagger but not with strong drink" — moral-spiritual intoxication without physical cause. The dullness is theological, not physical; it is the stupor that comes from sustained rejection of prophetic truth.
10: kî-nāsak ʿălêkem YHWH rûaḥ tardēmâ = "For YHWH has poured out upon you a spirit of deep sleep (tardēmâ, H8639)." Tardēmâ is the word used for Adam's sleep (Genesis 2:21) and Abraham's visionary trance (Genesis 15:12) — a divinely induced stupor. wayĕʿaṣṣēm ʾet-ʿênêkem ʾet-hannĕḇîʾîm = "and he has closed your eyes, the prophets" — the very people whose eyes were opened for vision have their eyes shut as judgment for spurning the vision. Paul cites this verse in Romans 11:8 ("God gave them a spirit of stupor, eyes that would not see and ears that would not hear").
11: wĕhāyâ lākem ḥăzôt hakkōl kidibĕrê hassēper heḥātûm = "And the vision of all this has become to you like the words of a sealed (ḥātam) scroll." The paradox: the prophetic revelation is inaccessible — either literally sealed (requiring the literate who say "I cannot read it, it is sealed," v11) or through illiteracy (v12). The word of God is present but unavailable to those in the stupor of v10.
12: wĕnātan hassēper ʾet ʾăšer lōʾ-yādaʿ sēper = "and the scroll is given to one who cannot read (lōʾ-yādaʿ sēper, lit. who does not know the scroll)." The illiterate man's response: "I cannot read." Both classes — the literate who say "it is sealed" and the illiterate who cannot read — are equally excluded from the prophetic vision. The seal is internal (the stupor of v10), not merely external (the wax seal of v11).
13:
yaʿan kî niggaš hāʿām hazzeh bĕpîw ûbĕśĕpātāyw kibbĕdûnî ûlibbô riḥaq mimmennî = "because this people draws near with their mouth (bĕpîw) and honors me with their lips (śĕpāhayim), but their heart (lēḇ) is far from me." Niggaš = to draw near (liturgically, as in priestly approach); the form of worship is present, the substance absent.

Jesus quotes this verse in Matthew 15:8-9 and Mark 7:6-7 as a direct critique of Pharisaic tradition that substitutes human precepts for the command of God. The precise Hebrew contrast (bĕpîw / libbô = mouth vs. heart) is the heart of Jesus's accusation: Corban practice honored God verbally while dishonoring parents practically. Isaiah's critique of empty cult becomes Jesus's critique of legal formalism — the structural problem is identical.
14:
lākēn hinnĕnî yôsip lĕhapliʾ ʾet-hāʿām hazzeh haplēʾ wāpele = "Therefore behold, I will again do wonderful things (hiplîʾ) with this people, wonderful and marvelous (haplēʾ wāpele)" — the divine wonder is not blessing here but judgment: the wisdom of the wise will perish. wĕʾābĕdâ ḥokmàt ḥăkāmāyw ûbînâ nĕḇōnāyw tistattār = "the wisdom (ḥokmâ) of its wise men will perish (ʾāḇad) and the understanding (bînâ) of its understanding men will be hidden (sātar)."

Paul directly quotes this verse in 1 Corinthians 1:19 ("I will destroy the wisdom of the wise, and the discernment of the discerning I will thwart") — applying it to the cross, which appears as foolishness to human wisdom but is the wisdom of God. The divine reversal of Isaiah 29:14 is enacted in the crucified Messiah: the very moment wisdom would declare the cross absurd, wisdom itself is overthrown.
15: hôy hammaʿămîqîm meYHWH lastar ʿēṣâ = "Woe to those who hide deep (māʿaq) from YHWH their plan (ʿēṣâ)" — plotting in secret as though the hidden God cannot see. wĕhāyâ bĕmaḥšāk maʿăśêhem = "and their deeds are in the dark" — the political machinations of the Judean leadership forming unauthorized alliances (with Egypt) while claiming to serve YHWH. wayyōʾmĕrû mî rōʾênû ûmî yôdĕʿēnû = "and they say, who sees us? who knows us?" — the epistemological arrogance of those who deny divine omniscience.
16: hăpkĕkem ʾim-kĕḥōmer hayôṣēr yeḥāšēḇ = "You turn things upside down! Shall the potter (yôṣēr) be regarded as the clay (ḥōmer)?" — the reversal of creator-creature distinction. kî-yōʾmar maʿăśeh lĕʿōśēhû lōʾ ʿāśānî = "as if the thing made (maʿăśeh) said of its maker (ʿōśeh): he did not make me." The potter/clay metaphor is the standard OT image of divine sovereignty over human creatures (cf. Jer 18; Romans 9:21). Paul cites Isaiah 29:16 and Isaiah 45:9 together in Romans 9:20-21 in his defense of God's sovereign election.
17: hălôʾ-ʿôd miʿaṭ mĕʿāṭ wĕšāḇ lĕḇānôn lakkarmel = "Is it not yet a very little while until Lebanon shall be turned into a fruitful field (karmel)?" — the eschatological reversal: the mighty forest becomes a garden, the garden becomes wild forest. wĕhakarmel layaʿar yeḥāšēḇ = "and the fruitful field shall be regarded as a forest" — when YHWH acts, conventional categories of abundance and wilderness exchange. The vision moves from judgment (vv1-16) toward restoration (vv17-24).
18: wĕšāmĕʿû bayyôm hahûʾ ḥĕrĕšîm diḇrê sēper = "And in that day the deaf (ḥĕrĕšîm) will hear the words of a scroll" — reversal of the sealed scroll (v11): what was inaccessible because of the stupor becomes accessible in the day of restoration. ûmēʾōpel ûmiḥōšek ʿênê ʿiwwĕrîm tirʾênâ = "and out of gloom and darkness the eyes of the blind will see" — the messianic signs that Jesus cited in Matthew 11:5 as proof of his identity (Isaiah 35:5-6; Isaiah 42:7) are anticipated here as eschatological markers.
19: wĕyāsĕpû ʿănāwîm bĕYHWH śimḥâ = "And the humble (ʿănāwîm) will increase their joy (śimḥâ) in YHWH." ʿĂnāwîm (H6041) = the poor, meek, lowly — the same word that appears in the Beatitudes context (Matthew 5:5: "blessed are the meek/humble"). wĕʾebyônê ʾādām biqĕdôš yiśrāʾēl yāgîlû = "and the needy (ʾebyôn) among mankind will exult in the Holy One of Israel." The eschatological reversal: the humbled become the joyful.
20: kî ʾāpes ʿārîṣ wĕkālâ lēṣ = "For the tyrant (ʿārîṣ) will be no more and the scoffer (lēṣ) will cease" — the two social enemies of the ʿănāwîm (the violent oppressor and the religious mocker) eliminated in the restoration. wĕnikrĕtû kol-šōqĕdê ʾāwen = "and all who watch for (šāqad) iniquity will be cut off" — the third category: the legalistic inquisitors who scrutinize others for violations they can exploit.
21: maḥăṭîʾê ʾādām bĕdāḇār = "those who cause a person to sin by a word (dāḇār)" — litigation tactics that trap people in verbal admission. ûlĕmôkîaḥ baššaʿar yĕqōšûn = "and who lay a snare for the one who reproves at the gate" — the gate (šaʿar) was the public legal forum where elders held court; targeting the judge/reprover there was a perversion of justice. wayyaṭṭû bĕtōhû ṣaddîq = "and by empty argument (tōhû) they turn aside the righteous" — tōhû = chaos/emptiness/nothingness; using insubstantial legal arguments to corrupt just decisions.
22: lākēn kōh-ʾāmar YHWH ʾel-bêt yaʿăqōb = "Therefore thus says YHWH to the house of Jacob" — the oracle of assurance that follows the reversal descriptions (vv17-21). ʾăšer pādâ ʾet-ʾAḇrāhām = "who redeemed (pādâ) Abraham" — an unusual application: Abraham as the one YHWH redeemed, usually applied to the Exodus. The connection of Abraham to redemption may relate to the Akedah (Gen 22) or to God's deliverance of Abraham from Ur. The point: the God who redeemed the father will restore the children. lōʾ-ʿattâ yēḇôš yaʿăqōb wĕlōʾ-ʿattâ pānāyw yeḥěwwārû = "Jacob shall not now be ashamed, nor shall his face now grow pale."
23: kî bĕrĕʾôtô yĕlādāyw maʿăśēh yādāy bĕqirbô yaqĕdîšû šĕmî = "For when he sees his children, the work of my hands, in his midst, they will sanctify (qiddaš) my name." Qiddaš (piel) = to treat as holy, to recognize and declare holiness. wĕhiqdîšû ʾet-qĕdôš yaʿăqōb wĕʾet-ʾĕlōhê yiśrāʾēl yaʿărîṣû = "and they will sanctify the Holy One of Jacob and will stand in awe of (ʿāraṣ) the God of Israel" — the eschatological goal of divine redemption: a community that recognizes and declares the holiness of God.
24: wĕyādĕʿû tōʿê-rûaḥ bînâ = "and those who err in spirit (tōʿê-rûaḥ) will come to understanding (bînâ)" — the restoration of the bînâ that was hidden (v14). wĕrōgĕnîm yilmĕdû lĕqāḥ = "and those who murmur (rāgan) will accept instruction (lāqaḥ)." Rāgan = to murmur, grumble against authority (used of Israel in the wilderness; cf. Numbers 14:2; Psalms 106:25); the murmurers who rejected prophetic instruction will receive it in the day of restoration. The spiritual inversion is complete: stupor → understanding; grumbling → instruction.
